costs: Legal and administrative redesign cost, including retraining expert committees and rewriting decree-level regulation
Risk of transitional confusion and disputes during phase-in, as seen in comparative reforms
content.equity_impact: Could reduce incentive-driven or definitionally volatile reclassification that disproportionately affects disadvantaged and Roma children if implemented with safeguards against misclassification, but comparative evidence also shows tiered systems can mask rather than resolve regional and socioeconomic disparities
expected_benefits: Potential reduction in diagnostic bottleneck and waiting lists by decoupling support access from full categorical determination, as Finland's model demonstrates support can be delivered without mandatory labeling
Reduced volatility from category-boundary incentive effects such as the documented BTMN/SNI grading-exemption asymmetry
content.goal: Redesign the SNI/BTMN category architecture and expert-committee gatekeeping process to reduce waiting-list bottlenecks, definitional volatility, and diagnosis-dependent access to support, informed by transferability-tested comparative models

implementation_steps.action: Commission a formal transferability assessment comparing Hungary's legal/administrative context to Wales' ALN and Finland's tiered-support reforms before drafting legislation
Draft and consult on amendments to Act CXC of 2011 and EMMI 15/2013 to pilot a tiered support framework in a subset of counties
Pilot restructured gatekeeping process with capacity monitoring to test waiting-list impact

mechanisms: Adoption of a tiered support structure decoupling access to support from formal categorical labeling, modeled on international evidence that non-categorical or tiered frameworks materially change measured identification rates (e.g., Wales' ALN transition and Finland's three-tier model)

Restructuring or augmenting the sole nationwide expert-committee gatekeeper to address documented multi-month waiting lists, informed by evidence that gatekeeping structure itself shapes measured prevalence independent of true need
risks: Comparative evidence shows non-categorical/tiered reforms produce large persistent regional variation even after redesign (Finland's 4%–13% municipal range), so redesign alone may not resolve Hungary's county-level disparities
Poland's experience shows rapid, non-consensual structural reform can produce severe implementation disruption and stakeholder backlash
content.title: Transition toward a non-categorical, tiered support model with restructured gatekeeping
